violations.title: Hardcoded JWT Secret Key in Source Code
violations.pattern_violated: Secrets Management — secrets must never be embedded in source code
violations.description: JWT_SECRET_KEY is defined as a C# string constant. DEFAULT_PASSWORD is also hardcoded. Both are committed to the repository. Any system using this key for token signing is vulnerable — the secret is exposed to anyone with repository read access.
violations.evidence: AuthorizationConstants.JWT_SECRET_KEY hardcoded string; AuthorizationConstants.DEFAULT_PASSWORD hardcoded string; both referenced from IdentityTokenClaimService.cs and AppIdentityDbContextSeed.cs
violations.impact: JWT tokens could be forged by any party with repository access. Seeded admin password is publicly known.
violations.remediation_recommendation: Move JWT_SECRET_KEY to environment variable or Azure Key Vault (IConfiguration). Use secrets manager / azd secrets for DEFAULT_PASSWORD seed data.

violations.title: Stub EmailSender in Production Code Path
violations.pattern_violated: Null Object pattern is acceptable only when explicitly named and documented; silent do-nothing implementations must not be registered in production DI
violations.description: EmailSender.SendEmailAsync() returns Task.CompletedTask without sending any email. The stub is registered as the IEmailSender implementation in DI with no conditional or environment check. All email notifications (password reset, order confirmation, account verification) silently do nothing.
violations.evidence: EmailSender.cs body: return Task.CompletedTask; — with TODO comment
violations.impact: Any feature relying on email (account verification, password reset) fails silently. No error is raised. Users cannot recover lost credentials.
violations.remediation_recommendation: Implement real email sending via SendGrid, Azure Communication Services, or SMTP. Use environment-conditional DI to register stub only in Development.

violations.title: God DbContext — CatalogContext Spans Multiple Bounded Contexts
violations.pattern_violated: DDD Bounded Context — each bounded context should have its own DbContext
violations.description: CatalogContext has 7 DbSets: Baskets, BasketItems, CatalogItems, CatalogBrands, CatalogTypes, Orders, OrderItems. This merges three bounded contexts (Catalog, Basket, Order) into a single EF context. Cross-aggregate queries and joins are trivially possible, bypassing aggregate root boundaries.
violations.evidence: CatalogContext: DbSet<Basket>, DbSet<BasketItem>, DbSet<CatalogItem>, DbSet<CatalogBrand>, DbSet<CatalogType>, DbSet<Order>, DbSet<OrderItem>
violations.impact: Any developer can write LINQ queries that cross aggregate boundaries directly on DbContext. Difficult to split into separate deployable services later. Single EF migration tracks all schema changes together.
violations.remediation_recommendation: Split into CatalogContext (CatalogItem, CatalogBrand, CatalogType), BasketContext (Basket, BasketItem), OrderContext (Order, OrderItem). Accept cross-context reads via IDs, not navigation properties.

violations.title: Repository Abstraction Bypass in BasketQueryService
violations.pattern_violated: Repository pattern — data access must go through IRepository<T> to maintain consistency and testability
violations.description: BasketQueryService.CountTotalBasketItems() injects CatalogContext directly to execute a SUM query. This bypasses the EfRepository<T> abstraction. While the performance rationale is valid (avoiding in-memory sum), it creates two data access patterns: EfRepository<T> and direct DbContext, making it inconsistent and harder to mock in tests.
violations.evidence: BasketQueryService constructor: CatalogContext _dbContext (direct injection, not IReadRepository<Basket>)
violations.impact: Test isolation for CountTotalBasketItems requires mocking CatalogContext instead of IReadRepository. Creates an inconsistent data access pattern.
violations.remediation_recommendation: Either document this as an intentional Query Service pattern (CQRS-lite), use IReadRepository with a custom raw SQL specification, or add a comment explaining the performance justification.

violations.title: Inconsistent API Endpoint Library Usage
violations.pattern_violated: Consistency principle — all endpoints in a single project should use the same endpoint implementation strategy
violations.description: CatalogBrandListEndpoint uses MinimalApi.Endpoint (MapEndpoint method on IEndpointRouteBuilder). AuthenticateEndpoint uses Ardalis.ApiEndpoints (EndpointBase base class with [HttpPost] attribute). Two different endpoint libraries are in use in the same project.
violations.evidence: CatalogBrandListEndpoint inherits from MinimalApi.Endpoint.IMinimalApiEndpoint; AuthenticateEndpoint inherits from Ardalis.ApiEndpoints.EndpointBaseAsync
violations.impact: Developer onboarding confusion. Inconsistent middleware, filter, and OpenAPI behavior across endpoints. Mixed testing approaches.
violations.remediation_recommendation: Standardize on one endpoint library. MinimalApi.Endpoint (ASP.NET Core minimal APIs) is the more modern approach.

violations.title: No Explicit Transaction Scope in Checkout Flow
violations.pattern_violated: Unit of Work — multi-step write operations must be wrapped in a transaction
violations.description: OrderService.CreateOrderAsync reads from Basket, reads from Catalog, then writes a new Order — but does not explicitly open a transaction. EF Core provides implicit transactions per SaveChanges(), but if the basket deletion step (not found in OrderService but expected after checkout) occurs in a separate operation, there is a window where the order is saved but the basket is not cleared.
violations.evidence: OrderService.CreateOrderAsync: multiple repository calls without explicit IDbContextTransaction or IUnitOfWork wrapper
violations.impact: Partial failure scenario: order created but basket not cleared — user could re-checkout the same basket, creating duplicate orders.
violations.remediation_recommendation: Wrap order creation and basket deletion in a single database transaction using IDbContextTransaction. Alternatively, implement outbox/event-driven pattern.

violations.title: 7-Day JWT Token Expiry with No Refresh Mechanism
violations.pattern_violated: JWT best practice — access tokens should have short TTL (15-60 min); use refresh tokens for long sessions
violations.description: JWT tokens are issued with a 7-day expiry. No refresh token mechanism is visible. A stolen JWT token remains valid for 7 days with no way to revoke it (no token blacklist, no Redis session store visible).
violations.impact: Compromised tokens are valid for 7 days. No logout / invalidation path exists.
violations.remediation_recommendation: Reduce access token TTL to 15-60 minutes. Implement refresh token flow. Add token revocation via allow-list or Redis.

violations.title: Dead Code — Buyer and PaymentMethod Aggregates
violations.pattern_violated: YAGNI — don't include code without active use
violations.description: Buyer and PaymentMethod entities are defined in the domain and registered in CatalogContext (DbSet<Buyer>) but have no visible service, repository, or controller references in the available Layer 1 artifacts. They appear to be leftover from planned but unimplemented payment functionality.
violations.evidence: CatalogContext has DbSet<Buyer> and DbSet<PaymentMethod>; no IRepository<Buyer>, no BuyerService, no BasketService or OrderService reference to Buyer found in Layer 1 artifacts
violations.impact: Unnecessary DB tables, schema migrations, and cognitive overhead. May confuse developers about intended payment flow.
violations.remediation_recommendation: Confirm dead code status and remove if no live callers. Or document intent and create tracking item for payment feature implementation.

violations.title: BlazorAdmin Cache Cleared on Every Startup
violations.pattern_violated: Cache warm-up — clearing cache on every application startup causes a cold-start performance spike
violations.description: BlazorAdmin Program.cs calls await localStorage.ClearAsync() during WebAssembly startup. This clears all browser-cached catalog data on every app start, forcing a full API round-trip on first page load regardless of whether the data is still fresh.
violations.evidence: src/BlazorAdmin/Program.cs: await localStorage.ClearAsync() — unconditional on startup
violations.impact: Every admin session begins with 3 parallel API calls to load brands, types, and items. No incremental or background cache warming.
violations.remediation_recommendation: Replace unconditional ClearAsync with TTL-based expiry check. Clear only stale items.
